Journal.Title: Journal of colloid and interface science

Article.ArticleTitle: Synergistic bimetallic enhancement in sulfur-doped spinel oxide cathodes derived from metal organic frameworks for direct borohydride-hydrogen peroxide fuel cells.

Abstract.AbstractText: The creation of extremely effective catalysts for the hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) reduction reaction (HPRR) is still a crucial problem influencing performance advancements in fuel cell systems with H2O2 as the cathodic oxidant. This study employs a hydrothermal method coupled with air calcination to construct in situ self-supported NiCo2O4-xSx on conductive nickel foam (NF) substrate. The metal organic framework (MOF)-derived framework provides abundant exposed active sites and rapid ion-transport pathways. Furthermore, sulfur (S) doping optimizes surface morphology to enlarge the electrochemically active surface area, while simultaneously modulates the Co/Ni valence states and introduces a high density of oxygen vacancies (OVs). These modifications are inferred to reconstruct the local electronic structure, which is beneficial to facilitate preferential HO2- adsorption and reduce the energy barrier for OO bond cleavage. The synergistic interaction between OVs and CoNi dual sites further promotes charge-transfer efficiency and reaction kinetics, resulting in markedly improved catalytic performance. Benefiting from these integrated structural and electronic modulation strategies, NiCo2O4-xSx/NF achieves a high current density of -853.97 mA·cm-2 at -0.8 V vs. Ag/AgCl in half-cell tests, consistent with its low activation energy (12.84 kJ·mol-1). When employed as the cathode in a direct borohydride‑hydrogen peroxide fuel cell (DBHPFC), it delivers a maximum power density of 75.26 mW·cm-2 at room temperature and sustains stable discharge for 15 h. Overall, the combination of MOF-derived framework, electronic structure optimization induced by S doping, and the cooperative advantages of CoNi dual-site catalysis with OVs endows NiCo2O4-xSx/NF with outstanding HPRR activity, highlighting its promise for high-performance DBHPFC cathodes.
